old_claim.note: Agriculture provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Agricultural surplus and productive economic contexts supported slave systems where owners expected economic gain from enslaved labor.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica says economic surplus was essential for slavery to flourish and discusses productive slavery in commodity-producing mines or plantations.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source frames surplus and commodity production as conditions that make slave systems economically useful.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Agriculture is retained as an economic scaling context for surplus and plantation-like production, not as a direct legal or technical enabler of slave property status.

ontology_before: Agriculture was a generic enabling edge for chattel slavery without specifying whether it was legal, economic, or chronological support.
ontology_after: Agriculture is a commercial/scaling context for surplus and productive labor extraction, while codified law carries the legal mechanism.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to nonproductive domestic slavery where agriculture and surplus were intentionally irrelevant.
A stronger source showed Old Babylonian legal chattel slavery did not rely on agricultural or surplus-producing economic context.
A separate surplus-production node replaced agriculture as the direct economic context.
short_reason: Agriculture supports the economic scaling context, not the legal mechanism.
